content:
# 2026-03-26

- [ ] Final review of [[budget-speech-fy2627]] before 6 PM board session
- [ ] Confirm Angela has printed board packets (all 7 copies)
- Met with Marcus this morning — he flagged that the revised expenditure table has a $120K rounding discrepancy; needs correction before tonight.
- Patricia called. She wants a 2-minute pre-meeting briefing at 5:30 PM. Confirmed.
- Reminder: tone tonight needs to be steady and solutions-focused. The community is watching.
- [ ] Send Tom Reilly the Mirai application checklist by EOD

# 2026-03-25

- Reviewed [[equity-audit-jan2026-summary]] with Grace — she flagged three schools still missing follow-up action plans.
- [ ] Follow up with Laura Kim on HR's staffing reduction timeline — need numbers before board session
- Union session tomorrow postponed per David Kowalski. Rescheduled to March 18.
- Called Tom Reilly re: Mirai STEM grant. He says the community partner letters of support need to be secured by end of July at the latest.
- Personal note: 父の誕生日 next week — do not schedule anything evening of March 17.

# 2026-03-31

- Morning: PAPEA planning call with alliance coordinators. Fall summit date locked — October 3.
- Refined [[mirai-grant-pitch]] opening paragraph. Needs to lead with student outcome data, not program description.
- [ ] Ask Diane Foster to review equity address draft for media sensitivity
- Budget gap now confirmed at $4.8M per Marcus's revised projections. No wiggle room in capital line.
- Grace reminded me: we still haven't publicly named the equity audit action lead at Westview Elementary.

# 2026-03-30

- Worked through [[equity-address-community]] draft — cut the opening anecdote, it read as deflection.
- Checked in with Angela: board packet deadline is March 10 noon.
- Quick call with Patricia re: board decorum expectations for the March 11 session. She's worried about public comment overflow.
- [ ] Remind Diane to prepare a press statement in case the vote on budget reductions goes sideways
- Laura Kim sent preliminary staffing impact numbers — 14 classified positions at risk.

# 2026-03-27

- End of week reflection: this has been a heavy month. The equity audit findings are still raw for a lot of staff.
- [[board-retreat-keynote]] outline is roughed in. Theme: 'Roots and Reach' — honoring where we've been, setting vision for where we go.
- [ ] Block two hours next week for deep work on Mirai grant narrative
- Marcus confirmed the FY2026-27 shortfall is structural, not a one-time anomaly. Need to say that plainly to the board.
- Grateful for Grace today — she handled the Westview situation with real care.

# Budget Speech FY2026-27 — Board Presentation
**Draft v3 — June 10, 2026**

---

## Opening
Good evening, board members, families, and community partners. Tonight I bring you a budget that reflects both the reality of our constraints and the depth of our commitments.

I will not minimize what we are facing. A $4.8 million structural shortfall requires honest, difficult choices. But I want you to know: every reduction in this proposal was made through the lens of what is best for students.

## Core Message
- We have closed $2.1M through administrative consolidation and energy savings.
- The remaining $2.7M requires reductions in force — primarily classified support positions.
- We have preserved every classroom teacher currently under contract.
- No school will close. No program serving our highest-need students will be eliminated.

## The Mirai STEM Grant
We are aggressively pursuing the Mirai Foundation STEM grant — $1.2M over three years — with a deadline of November 16. If awarded, this funding directly offsets cuts to our middle school science elective block.

## Closing
I became a superintendent because I believe in the transformative power of public education. That belief does not waver in lean years. It sharpens.

I ask for your vote tonight — not because this budget is easy, but because it is honest, equitable, and forward-looking.

---
*[Note to self: pause after 'every reduction' — let that land. Do not rush the closing.]*

# Equity Address — Community Forum
**Draft v2 — June 8, 2026**

---

## Purpose
Address the January 2026 equity audit findings directly at the community forum. Audience: parents, teachers, community advocates, some press.

## Key Points
1. The audit findings are not a surprise to us — but they confirm what our students of color and students with disabilities have been telling us.
2. We released the full report in January because transparency is non-negotiable. Some districts bury these. We did not.
3. Concrete actions already underway:
   - Revised discipline matrix adopted district-wide (effective March 1)
   - Implicit bias training required for all new hires starting July 2026
   - Equity leads appointed at 9 of 14 schools (remaining 5 by September)
4. What we still owe you: a public accountability dashboard, updated quarterly.

## Tone Guidance
- Do not be defensive. The audit is a tool, not a verdict.
- Acknowledge harm where harm occurred. Don't hedge.
- Signal that this work is ongoing, not a checkbox.

## Closing Line (draft)
"Equity is not an initiative with an end date. It is the standard by which we measure everything else we do."

---
*[Note: Diane reviewed March 9 — she flagged 'implicit bias' framing as potentially charged with certain parent groups. Consider 'culturally responsive practices training' as softer substitute. Thinking about it.]*

# Union Negotiation Remarks — June Session
**Draft — pre-meeting notes, NOT a formal speech**

---

## Context
Session with David Kowalski and CRUSD Teachers Association bargaining team. Budget pressure is real; this is the third session this spring.

## My Opening Position
- I respect this union. I was a classroom teacher. I know what the contract means to the people across that table.
- I am asking for flexibility on step increases for FY2026-27 — a one-year freeze at current step, not a cut.
- In exchange: full preservation of prep periods, no changes to class size caps, and a joint task force on workload equity.

## What David Wants (based on last session)
- Cost-of-living adjustment (3.2% ask)
- Additional planning time for special education co-teachers
- Transparency on administrator salary structure

## Where I Can Move
- Open to a smaller COLA (1.5% if we close grant funding by Oct)
- Willing to pilot expanded co-teacher prep time at two schools
- Will provide admin salary band summary — not individual salaries

## Red Lines
- Cannot agree to step increases AND COLA in same year given $4.8M gap
- Cannot reduce classified support staff AND increase teacher load — that's a trap

## Tone
David is a tough negotiator but he's not bad faith. Treat him as a partner under pressure, not an adversary.

# Mirai STEM Grant Pitch — Stakeholder Preview
**Draft — June 9, 2026 | Deadline: Nov 16, 2026**

---

## Elevator Version (90 seconds)
Cascade Ridge USD serves 11,400 students across 14 schools. Our middle school STEM pipeline has a measurable equity gap: girls and students from our Latino and Pacific Islander communities enroll in advanced science electives at half the rate of their peers. The Mirai grant would fund three years of targeted curriculum redesign, teacher professional development, and near-peer mentorship — directly addressing that gap with evidence-based practices.

## Headline Data Points
- 43% of CRUSD 8th graders do not take any science elective beyond the state requirement
- That number rises to 61% among students qualifying for free/reduced lunch
- Comparable districts that implemented Mirai-funded STEM cohorts saw a 28% increase in advanced science enrollment within two years

## What We're Asking For
- $1.2M over 3 years
- Funds: curriculum specialists (2 FTE), summer intensive program, materials, and family engagement coordinator

## Tom Reilly's Role
Tom is coordinating the application. He needs:
- [ ] Signed letters of support from 3 community partners (due October 31)
- [ ] Board resolution of endorsement (target: October board meeting)
- [ ] Baseline data report from Laura Kim's department

---
*[This pitch version is for Patricia and board members — not the foundation itself. Keep it local and human.]*

# Board Retreat Keynote — August 2026
**Outline Draft — June 6, 2026**

---

## Theme: Roots and Reach
*Honoring where we've come from. Setting vision for where we go.*

## Section 1: Roots (10 min)
- Brief history of CRUSD — founded 1947, consolidated 1962, grew through Portland's demographic shift in the 1990s
- What has stayed constant: commitment to every student, in every neighborhood
- Personal note: my family's story is woven into public schools. My mother taught ESL in this district for 22 years.

## Section 2: Where We Stand (15 min)
- Honest assessment of FY2026-27 — the shortfall, the choices we've made
- Equity audit findings and where we are in the response
- Union negotiations: status and spirit of partnership

## Section 3: Reach (20 min)
- Mirai STEM grant as a strategic bet on our next generation
- PAPEA alliance as a model for regional solidarity among equity-focused districts
- Vision: by 2030, every CRUSD student graduates with a documented pathway — academic, technical, or both

## Closing
We are not managing a decline. We are building something. The budget is tight but the mission is not diminished. I need this board fully committed — not despite the difficulty, but because of it.

---
*[August date TBD — Angela is coordinating with Patricia on venue. Budget for retreat not to exceed $3,200.]*

# PAPEA Summit Remarks — Fall 2026
**Notes — June 4, 2026 | Summit Date: October 3, 2026**

---

## PAPEA Context
Portland Area Public Education Alliance — coalition of 8 neighboring districts sharing equity frameworks and policy advocacy capacity. I joined the steering committee in 2025.

## Message for the Summit
1. Budget austerity is a regional challenge — we cannot let it fracture the solidarity that makes PAPEA valuable.
2. The equity audit we released in January is a model other districts can adapt. Offer to share our framework.
3. Advocate for a collective ask to the state legislature: restore the rural/urban equity weighting in the school funding formula.

## Key Talking Points
- PAPEA districts collectively serve 87,000 students — that is a political constituency, not just a network
- We should be commissioning joint research, not reinventing the wheel at each district separately
- The Mirai grant model could be replicated across the alliance if we develop a shared application template

## Audience
Superintendents, school board members, community advocates from 8 districts. Some state legislative staffers expected.

---
*[Tone: collegial and energizing. I'm not the most senior person in the room — be a peer, not a keynote star.]*

# Board Prep — June Budget Session
**June 11, 2026 | Meeting starts 6:30 PM**

---

## Logistics
- Angela confirmed: room setup done by 5:45 PM
- Patricia wants pre-brief at 5:30 PM (brief: 10 min max)
- Public comment period: up to 30 speakers registered as of this morning
- Marcus Bell presenting the financial overview (slides 1-12), then I take over for the policy framing (slides 13-22)

## Anticipated Tough Questions
1. **"Why are classified staff bearing the brunt?"**
   Response: We preserved all classroom teacher positions. Classified reductions were made where student-contact impact was lowest. Still painful — own that.

2. **"What's the contingency if the Mirai grant doesn't come through?"**
   Response: We have a Plan B — phased reduction in the middle school elective block over two years rather than one. Buys time without eliminating the program.

3. **"Why wasn't the community consulted more?"**
   Response: Two community forums held in April. Input was incorporated — the library aide position was restored after forum feedback. But I hear the desire for more. Commit to earlier engagement in FY2027-28 cycle.

## Board Member Read
- Patricia Odom: supportive, wants the vote to move cleanly. Watch for her signaling.
- Marcus's presentation: make sure he doesn't get too deep in the weeds on fund accounting — the public will disengage.
- Board member Chen: likely to push on equity framing of reductions. Have equity audit slide ready.

## My Mental State
- [ ] Deep breath before I walk in. This is hard but it is right.
- The community deserves honesty more than they deserve comfort.

# Board Prep — Equity Audit Presentation
**Prepared: June 3, 2026**

---

## Purpose
Present January 2026 equity audit findings and the district's response plan to the full board. Not a crisis — this is accountability in action.

## Audit Key Findings (summary)
1. Discipline disparity: Black students suspended at 3.1x the rate of white students (district average)
2. Advanced course enrollment: significant underrepresentation of Latino, Pacific Islander, and students with IEPs
3. Teacher diversity: 78% of CRUSD teachers identify as white; district student population is 52% students of color
4. Facilities equity: three Title I schools scored lowest on physical environment quality index

## District Response (as of June 2026)
- Revised discipline matrix: adopted, all principals trained
- Equity leads: 9 of 14 schools designated (5 remaining by September)
- Advanced course access: pilot open-enrollment policy at two high schools for Fall 2026
- Hiring: revised job postings and partnership with HBCUs and HSIs for teacher pipeline
- Facilities: capital request submitted for Garfield and Lincoln ES; pending state match

## Board Questions to Prepare For
- "When will we see the discipline numbers move?" — Answer: tracking quarterly; first meaningful data point December 2026.
- "How are we measuring equity lead effectiveness?" — Answer: rubric in development with Grace and Laura, due August.

## Frame
This audit is not a judgment on individual teachers or principals. It is a systems-level mirror. Our job is to use it.

# Board Prep — October Meeting
**Preliminary notes — June 1, 2026**

---

## Key Items Expected on October Agenda
1. Mirai STEM grant board resolution of endorsement (Tom Reilly presenting)
2. Q1 FY2026-27 budget variance report (Marcus Bell)
3. Equity audit 6-month progress update
4. Union contract status update (if not resolved by then)
5. PAPEA summit debrief

## Prep Priorities (to do before October)
- [ ] Confirm Mirai grant application is on track — Tom should have a draft to me by December 1
- [ ] Get union contract to a tentative agreement before October if at all possible
- [ ] Commission equity dashboard prototype — want to show the board something concrete
- [ ] Coordinate with Diane on communication plan around Q1 variance report if numbers are worse than projected

## Notes
- Patricia has asked for a "no surprises" rule — anything significant should be briefed to her before it hits the public agenda.
- October is also PAPEA summit month — schedule is going to be compressed. Brief Angela.

# Talking Points — Patricia Odom (Board President)
**Updated June 10, 2026**

---

## Relationship Notes
Patricia has been board president for four years. She is politically cautious and consensus-oriented. She dislikes surprises and rewards preparation. Our relationship is good — she backed my hiring.

## For March 11 Pre-Brief
- Lead with: "The vote tonight is straightforward — I have the four votes we need. My concern is the public comment period."
- Reassure her: Diane has a press statement ready. If public comment gets heated, we let people speak without responding from the dais.
- Flag: the rounding discrepancy in the expenditure table was corrected this afternoon (Marcus confirmed at 2 PM).

## Ongoing Dynamics
- She supports the Mirai grant but wants a clearer ROI narrative — "what does this get us in three years?"
- On the union: she wants resolution before October board meeting. She's worried about bad press.
- She has asked twice about teacher diversity hiring. She's right to push — update her on the HBCU/HSI pipeline work.

## Never Say to Patricia
- "I'll keep you posted" without a specific follow-up date
- Anything that sounds like I'm running around the board, even if I'm not

# Talking Points — David Kowalski (Union President)
**Updated June 9, 2026**

---

## Relationship Notes
David has been union president for six years. He's a former high school history teacher — Eastridge HS. Smart, principled, occasionally combative but fundamentally interested in fair outcomes. He doesn't respect vagueness.

## For March 18 Rescheduled Session
- Open with acknowledgment that the postponement was disruptive — own it, even though the schedule conflict was his.
- Be direct about the one-year step freeze ask. Don't bury it.
- Offer the joint workload equity task force as a genuine partnership, not a consolation prize.

## His Likely Openings
- COLA at 3.2%: prepared to counter at 1.5% contingent on grant outcome by October
- Co-teacher prep time: willing to pilot, but needs a real commitment date, not "we'll explore"
- Admin salary transparency: will provide band summary — I've cleared this with Laura

## What I Respect About David
He genuinely cares about teacher retention. The district loses 18% of new teachers in years 1-3. That's a problem we share. Find the places where our interests converge and build from there.

## Red Lines (mine)
- Step freeze is non-negotiable in context of $4.8M gap — but frame it as one-year only, with a written commitment to revisit in FY2027-28
- Cannot make a COLA promise that isn't funded

# Talking Points — Marcus Bell (CFO)
**Updated June 8, 2026**

---

## Relationship Notes
Marcus joined CRUSD 18 months ago from Multnomah County budget office. Precise, conservative, sometimes overly technical in public-facing communications. I trust his numbers — I need him to translate them.

## For Board Presentation Coordination
- His slides 1-12: I need him to spend no more than 18 minutes. The board and public glaze over after that.
- Key ask: lead with the $4.8M figure on slide 2, not slide 9. Don't build to it. Own it up front.
- Ensure the Mirai grant contingency language is on slide 11 — Patricia specifically asked for this.

## Ongoing Issues
- March 11 rounding discrepancy: corrected — Marcus confirmed fix at 2 PM today.
- Q1 variance report: ask Marcus to flag any mid-year adjustments that may land differently than projected. I want no surprises in October.
- Reserve fund status: we are at 3.1% — below the board-adopted 4% minimum. This will come up. Talking point: our restoration plan targets 4% by end of FY2027-28.

## Communication Coaching (gentle)
Marcus sometimes uses fund accounting jargon in public settings. Remind him before March 11: if you say 'object code' or 'encumbrance' in the public session, translate immediately.

# Talking Points — Dr. Grace Whitmore (Deputy Superintendent)
**Updated June 7, 2026**

---

## Relationship Notes
Grace is my closest working partner and the most operationally capable person on my team. She manages the school principals directly. I trust her judgment completely — but I sometimes need to push her to be more assertive in board-facing contexts.

## Current Priorities for Grace
1. Equity audit follow-up: she owns the action plan implementation. As of March 9, three schools still missing updated plans — she's on it.
2. Mirai grant: she is the internal champion. Make sure she and Tom Reilly are in sync.
3. Union: keep her briefed but do not pull her into the bargaining room unless I specifically ask.

## For Ongoing Check-ins
- Weekly 30-min sync on Tuesdays — protect this time
- She flagged potential principal vacancy at Garfield ES — succession planning conversation needed before July
- Ask her to develop a one-page summary of equity lead progress by August board meeting

## Personal Note
Grace tends to understate her own contributions. When she does good work — like the Westview situation this week — name it publicly. She responds well to recognition and it models the culture I want.

# Talking Points — Board Retreat Planning
**Updated June 5, 2026**

---

## Purpose of Retreat (August 2026, date TBD)
Annual board-superintendent planning retreat. This year's focus: FY2026-27 priorities and long-range vision through 2030.

## Agenda Shape I'm Proposing
- Morning: financial reality check + honest debrief on FY2025-26 decisions
- Midday: equity audit response — where are we, what does success look like
- Afternoon: 2030 vision setting — what kind of district do we want to be?

## Tone Guidance
- This should not feel like a board meeting with a catered lunch. Create space for real conversation.
- I want to spend at least 90 minutes in open dialogue — no slides, just talking.
- Consider bringing in a facilitator for the afternoon visioning session.

## Logistical Notes
- Angela is coordinating venue. Budget cap: $3,200.
- Aim for off-site (not district HQ) — psychological distance matters.
- Invite the board but not the public — this is a working session.

## Key Message for the Retreat
I need the board to understand: the $4.8M shortfall is not a one-year problem we're solving. It is a structural signal about how we are funded and what we are asked to do. The retreat is where we decide how to respond strategically, not just operationally.

- Ask Angela to block March 17 evening — 父の誕生日
- Tom Reilly needs reminder: community partner letters of support due October 31
- Diane: check media sensitivity on 'implicit bias' language in equity address
- Follow up with Laura Kim — 14 classified positions at risk, need final list before March 11
- Idea for retreat: bring in external facilitator for afternoon visioning block
- Patricia: she wants 'no surprises' — brief her on anything significant before it hits the public agenda
- Check reserve fund narrative for board session — currently at 3.1%, below 4% target

# Mirai STEM Grant — Requirements & Timeline
**Reference — last reviewed May 28, 2026**

---

## Funder
Mirai Foundation — Portland-based family foundation focused on STEM equity in K-12 public education.

## Grant Overview
- Award amount: up to $1.2M over 3 years
- Eligibility: public school districts in Oregon and Washington with demonstrated equity gap in STEM enrollment
- Application deadline: **November 16, 2026**
- Award notification: February 2027
- Grant period: July 2027 – June 2030

## Application Requirements
1. Needs assessment with district-level data (enrollment, outcome gaps)
2. Program design narrative (curriculum, professional development, mentorship)
3. Budget with year-by-year breakdown
4. Letters of support: minimum 3 community partners (due with application)
5. Board resolution of endorsement
6. Evaluation plan with measurable outcomes

## CRUSD Internal Timeline
| Milestone | Target Date |
|---|---|
| Baseline data report finalized | Aug 1, 2026 |
| Community partner letters secured | Jul 31, 2026 |
| Draft narrative to Yumi for review | Sep 1, 2026 |
| Board resolution of endorsement | Oct board meeting |
| Final application submitted | Nov 10, 2026 |

## Tom Reilly's Contact Notes
- Tom is CRUSD's Title IV coordinator — he owns this application.
- He has a strong relationship with the Mirai program officer (Keiko Tanaka).
- Previous CRUSD application (2023) was declined — cited lack of community partnership depth. Do not repeat that mistake.

# Equity Audit January 2026 — Key Findings
**Reference — last reviewed May 20, 2026**

---

## Audit Commissioned By
CRUSD Board of Education, September 2025. Conducted by Portland Equity Consulting Group. Released publicly January 14, 2026.

## Methodology
Document review, staff surveys (n=412), student focus groups (n=87), principal interviews, analysis of 5 years of district administrative data.

## Finding 1: Discipline Disparities
- Black students: suspended at 3.1x the rate of white students
- Students with IEPs: suspended at 2.4x the rate of non-IEP peers
- Chronic absenteeism correlated with discipline referral history at 7 of 14 schools

## Finding 2: Advanced Course Access
- AP/honors enrollment: 67% white students, 14% Latino students, 8% Pacific Islander students (district demographics: 48% white, 21% Latino, 9% Pacific Islander)
- Identified cause: informal teacher recommendation gating — no formal open-enrollment policy
- Middle school pipeline: lack of advanced math exposure in 6th grade predicts non-enrollment in high school AP

## Finding 3: Teacher Workforce
- 78% of certified staff identify as white
- 12% Latino, 4% Black, 3% Asian American, 3% other
- New teacher retention (years 1-3): 82% white teachers, 67% teachers of color

## Finding 4: Facilities Equity
- Garfield ES, Lincoln ES, Westview MS: lowest facility quality index scores
- All three are Title I schools
- Deferred maintenance estimated at $4.2M across these three sites

## District Response Actions (as of June 2026)
- [ ] Revised discipline matrix — COMPLETE (March 2026)
- [ ] Equity leads at all 14 schools — IN PROGRESS (9/14 complete)
- [ ] Open-enrollment pilot for advanced courses — PLANNED (Fall 2026)
- [ ] Teacher diversity hiring initiative — UNDERWAY
- [ ] Facilities capital request — SUBMITTED (state match pending)
- [ ] Accountability dashboard — IN DEVELOPMENT

# Board prep — FY2027 budget shortfall

- Frame by stakeholder impact: union, board, east-side parents.
- Lead with the data, close with the kid it serves.
- Steady tone. Calm carries further than volume.

# Talking points — Mirai STEM grant application

- One sentence the board can repeat.
- Anticipate Patricia's fiscal-discipline push.
- Keep David at the table on Mirai STEM grant application.

# Speech draft — January equity audit response

Public institutions are the scaffolding of a just society. The people inside them deserve more respect than the culture provides.

# Board Readiness Context — 22,000 Students, 31 Schools, 2,800 Employees
**Draft note for the $4.8M shortfall conversation**

## Population scale
- CRUSD serves approximately **22,000 students** across **31 schools** with **~2,800 employees**.
- Per-student operating cost in FY2025-26 lands near $14,800; the $4.8M shortfall is ~1.5% of the operating base.
- Framing the shortfall against 22,000 students (not against a headline number) keeps stakeholder impact honest.

## Three-lever posture
- Option A (administrative consolidation + facilities-cost shift under bond) is my defensible option.
- Option B (facilities bond alone) frees operating capacity but leaves a residual against the $4.8M target.
- Option C (instructional-staff reductions) is my last resort, not my first.

## Reconciliation reminders
- Salesforce pledge total is the CRM stage figure; Xero receipts are the cash-in-hand truth.
- Confluence staffing snapshot is stale (FY2024-25); use the live HR / greenhouse pipeline for vacancies.
- Mixpanel family-portal usage is the authoritative east-side access surface; Google Analytics is the flattering proxy.
- Zendesk raw ticket queue is the parent-complaint truth; recycled summaries elsewhere are for narrative, not for the board.
